items.text: You have power over your mind — not outside events. Realize this, and you will find strength.
Between stimulus and response there is a space. In that space is our power to choose our response.
He who has a why to live for can bear almost any how.
The obstacle in the path becomes the path. Never forget, within every obstacle is an opportunity to improve our condition.
You cannot stop the waves, but you can learn to surf.
What we do now echoes in eternity.
We suffer more often in imagination than in reality.
If you are distressed by anything external, the pain is not due to the thing itself, but to your estimate of it; and this you have the power to revoke at any moment.
You are the sky. Everything else — it's just the weather.
First, say to yourself what you would be; and then do what you have to do.
Do not pray for an easy life, pray for the strength to endure a difficult one.
The wound is the place where the Light enters you.

5–4–3–2–1. Look around and name 5 things you can see. Then 4 things you can feel touching your body. Then 3 things you can hear. Then 2 things you can smell. Then 1 thing you can taste. Take it slow — the point is the noticing.

Feet on the floor. Press them down. Feel the ground holding you up — it has been doing that this whole time. Notice the weight of your body in the chair, the temperature of the air, the back of your shirt against your skin. You are here. You are held.

Body scan, top down. Forehead — soften it. Jaw — unclench. Shoulders — drop them an inch. Chest — let it open. Belly — let it be soft. Hands — unfurl them. Notice anywhere you were holding tension you didn't know about.

Name it to tame it. Out loud or in your head: "I'm noticing anxiety. I'm noticing a tight chest. I'm noticing the thought that I'm going to fail." Naming the experience moves it from the driver's seat to the passenger seat.

Orient. Slowly turn your head left, then right, then up, then down. Let your eyes wander to whatever they want to land on. This signals your nervous system that you're safe in your environment — it's a built-in reset.

Cold water reset. Splash cold water on your face, or hold an ice cube in your palm for 30 seconds, or run wrists under cold tap. The cold triggers the dive reflex — heart rate drops, parasympathetic kicks in. Fastest physiological reset there is.

Hand on heart. Place one hand on your chest, the other on your belly. Feel the rise and fall. Say to yourself: "This is a moment of suffering. Suffering is part of being human. May I be kind to myself right now."
Box breathing. Inhale 4 → hold 4 → exhale 4 → hold 4. Repeat for 8 rounds. Used by Navy SEALs to calm down before high-stakes moments.
4-7-8. Inhale through nose for 4. Hold for 7. Exhale slowly through mouth for 8. Repeat 4 times. The long exhale is the active ingredient — it tells your nervous system the threat has passed.

Physiological sigh. Two quick inhales through your nose (a big one, then a small top-up), then one long slow exhale through your mouth. Repeat 1–3 times. Fastest way to lower stress in real time — works in under 30 seconds.
Coherent breathing. Inhale 5, exhale 5. Just that. Five rounds. Brings heart rate variability up and signals safety to your nervous system. The simplest breath practice that exists.
Extended exhale. Inhale through your nose for a count of 4. Exhale through your nose or pursed lips for a count of 8. Whatever the inhale is, double the exhale. Six rounds.
What would you tell a friend who was feeling exactly this right now? Now — say that to yourself. You're allowed to be on your own side.
Will this matter in 10 minutes? In 10 days? In 10 months? In 10 years? Most things scale down quickly when you zoom out.
Two columns: what's in my control, what's not. Anything in the second column — set it down, just for now. You can pick it up later if you want. Right now, only act on column one.
You are not your thoughts. Try this: "I am having the thought that I am failing." Notice the gap that opens up. The thought is a passenger. You're the one driving.
What would the version of me I'm trying to become do in this moment? Don't think about who you are. Think about who you're becoming. Act from there.
Name three values that matter to you. Just three. Now: which of them does the next 10 minutes serve? You don't need to feel motivated. You need to act in line with what you care about.
This feeling will not last. It never has. Sit with it the way you'd sit with a friend who's having a hard time. You don't have to fix it. You just have to be there.

What is this feeling actually trying to tell me? Anger often points to a violated value. Anxiety often points to something I care about. Sadness often points to something I've lost. Listen before you try to make it go away.
Stand up. Walk outside, even if it's just to the front step or window. 60 seconds of fresh air and a different visual field. That's the whole assignment.
Drink a full glass of water. Slowly. Notice the temperature, the swallow, the breath after. Most low-energy moments have a dehydration component.
10 pushups. Or 20 air squats. Or 30 jumping jacks. Pick one. Do it now. Movement is the fastest legal mood-changer there is.
Write one sentence — just one — about how you're actually feeling right now. No editing. No audience. Then close the app. The act of putting it into words is the medicine.
Text one person who matters to you. "Thinking of you" is enough. You're allowed to need connection. They're allowed to want to hear from you.
Tidy one thing. One. The corner of the desk, the bedside table, the coffee mug. Action precedes motivation — the small win opens the door.
Record a 30-second voice memo to yourself. Talk through what's going on. Then play it back. Hearing your own voice say the thing changes its weight.
Sunlight on your face for 2 minutes. If it's daytime, go to a window or step outside. If it's not, switch on a bright light and look at something 6+ feet away. Light entrains the system.
